agent1_snippets.title: Ptosis Correction -- Introduction -- Myasthenia gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by the presence of antibodies to acetylcholine receptors located at the neuromuscular endplates of voluntary muscles. [3] This leads to decreased action of acetylcholine which results in muscle weakness and fatigue. Myasthenia may be generalized or localized to the eye (ocular myasthenia). The most common presenting feature is variable ptosis associated with diplopia. Symptoms may be unilateral or bilateral. Patients with myasthenia initially have a good levator function. Prolonged upgaze will cause a worsening of ptosis in these patients due to muscle fatigue. Cogan lid twitch sign: Rapid saccadic eye movements from downgaze to primary position results in rapid upshoot of the lid followed by a gradual drop to the primary position. Other tests which help confirm the diagnosis include ice test, serum acetylcholine receptor antibody assay, single fiber electromyography, and repetitive nerve stimulation test.

agent1_snippets.title: Edrophonium (Archived) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Edrophonium was classically used for differentiation of cholinergic crisis from the myasthenic crisis. [2] Edrophonium briefly ameliorated the symptoms of MG by increasing the amount of acetylcholine in the NMJ synapses. The increased levels of acetylcholine in the NMJ resulted in brief improvements in skeletal and muscular strength in MG patients. Edrophonium's historical use was in MG patients with ptosis or extraocular muscle weaknesses with immediate improvement upon drug administration. As of 2018, the FDA discontinued edrophonium, which is no longer available in the United States due to its high rate of false-positive results and the development of serological antibody testing as the gold standard for diagnosing MG. [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Edrophonium (Tensilon) Test: Edrophonium is a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that increases the availability of ACh in the NMJ. This is particularly useful for ocular MG, where electrophysiologic testing cannot be performed. It is administered intravenously, and the patient is observed for improvement in the symptoms of ptosis or diplopia. It has a sensitivity of 71% to 95% for MG diagnosis. [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Diplopia -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: If fatiguability, variability, decreased orbicularis strength, or any other symptoms of ocular or generalized myasthenia gravis are present, acetylcholine receptor antibody testing should be performed, although its sensitivity is only about 50% to 70%. [22] If the diagnosis is strongly suspected despite the negative acetylcholine receptor antibody titers, a single fiber electromyogram of the orbicularis muscle should be performed, as in the hands of an experienced operator, it has a sensitivity of about 95% in diagnosing abnormalities specific to ocular myasthenia. [23]

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Related Testing
agent1_snippets.content: During RNS testing, a sequence of supramaximal intensity impulses is applied to the nerve, and the response is recorded from the corresponding muscle. A decrement or increment in response indicates a specific NMJ pathology. A decremental response to RNS is commonly seen in clinical muscle fatigue and weakness. Abnormal RNS results are observed in more than 50% to 70% of generalized myasthenia gravis patients, but it is often normal in those with only ocular myasthenia gravis. Usually, a 10% decrement on slow RNS (2-3 Hz) is typically observed in patients with myasthenia gravis. In those with ocular myasthenia gravis, electromyographic (EMG) abnormalities may only be detectable in the facial muscles.

agent1_snippets.title: Edrophonium (Archived) -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Edrophonium is a reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor with rapid onset and short duration of action resulting in an increase of acetylcholine in the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). [1] Since the early 1930s, it has been a diagnostic tool for myasthenia gravis (MG). MG is a neuromuscular disorder characterized by muscular weakness due to antibody production that inhibits or destroys post-synaptic nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in the NMJ. Muscle weakness in MG presents as ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria, and dysphagia and can progress to fatal respiratory depression in critically ill patients. For many years, edrophonium, marketed as the Tensilon test, was FDA-approved to be utilized to diagnose MG.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Pathophysiology -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis typically presents with double vision (diplopia), drooping of the upper eyelids (ptosis), difficulty speaking (dysarthria), difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), and generalized muscle fatigue. Symptoms are usually mildest in the morning and worsen throughout the day as ACh availability at the postsynaptic membrane decreases with continued muscle activity. In progressive cases, muscle weakness may worsen over time, potentially leading to a myasthenic crisis and, in severe cases, death. A decrement of more than 10% on RNS testing is a diagnostic criterion for myasthenia gravis, indicating depletion of functional ACh in the synaptic cleft. [6] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Immediate Hypersensitivity Reactions (Archived) -- Introduction -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder caused by antibodies to post-synaptic acetylcholine receptors that interfere with neuromuscular transmission. It is characterized by extreme muscular fatigue, double vision, bilateral ptosis, deconjugate eye movements, difficulty swallowing, and weakness in the upper arms. Babies born to mothers with myasthenia gravis can have transient muscle weakness due to pathogenic IgG antibodies that cross the placenta. [14]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Extraocular Muscle Weakness: Around 85% of patients will have this on the initial presentation. Common patient complaints include diplopia, ptosis, or both. These symptoms can progress and cause generalized MG involving the bulbar, axial, and limb muscles in 50% of patients in two years. [10] Bulbar Muscle Weakness: This can be the initial presentation in 15% of patients and causes symptoms like difficulty chewing or frequent choking, dysphagia, hoarseness, and dysarthria. [10] The involvement of facial muscles causes an expressionless face, and neck muscle involvement causes a dropped-head syndrome. Limb Weakness: This usually involves the proximal muscles more than distal muscles, with the upper limbs more affected than the lower limbs. Myasthenic crisis: It is due to the involvement of intercostal muscles and diaphragm and is a medical emergency.

The pupils, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory examinations are normal.

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Ice-pack Test: When edrophonium testing is contraindicated, an ice-pack test can be performed. This test requires an ice-pack placed over the eye for 2-5 minutes. Then, an assessment for any improvement in ptosis is done. This test cannot be used for the evaluation of extraocular muscles.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Synapse -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia Gravis is an auto-immune disease process that causes muscle weakness that usually presents in a descending fashion. It can cause ptosis, diminished facial expression, respiratory depression, and other signs/symptoms of weakness. In general, it is worse after activity and better with rest. The pathogenesis of myasthenia gravis involves diminished communication between the neuron and the muscle at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). This is because antibodies either block or destroy the acetylcholine receptors at the NMJ, preventing the ACh from binding and depolarizing the muscle, inhibiting contraction. These antibodies block step 3 (receptor activation) of the synaptic communication pathway.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Skeletal Muscle -- Pathophysiology -- Diseases Affecting the Neuromuscular Junction
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia Gravis is an autoimmune disorder that results from antibodies to the ACh receptors of the neuromuscular junction. These antibodies prevent ACh from binding and decrease the depolarization transmitted to the muscle cell. As repetitive use consumes ACh stores, lower concentrations of ACh released into the NMJ cannot saturate the binding sites and produce an action potential within the muscle. This presents as a variable weakness in the patient that is worse with use and better with rest. Often, the extraocular muscles are the first muscles affected in the course of the disease. Edrophonium, a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, can be used to diagnose myasthenia gravis. When administered, edrophonium prolongs the action of ACh at the NMJ and prevents muscle fatigue for a short time. [13]

agent1_snippets.title: Ptosis(Archived) -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: If myasthenia gravis is suspected, blood serum is tested for acetylcholine receptor antibodies, which are responsible for this autoimmune disorder. However, these are positive only in 50% of patients with solely ocular myasthenia. [9] Antistriated muscle antibodies and muscle-specific tyrosine kinase levels are also checked when myasthenia is highly suspected.

agent1_snippets.title: Ptosis Correction -- Introduction -- Specific examination of ptosis
agent1_snippets.content: 10. Phenylephrine test: It is a useful test in patients with mild ptosis or ptosis due to Horner syndrome; instill 2.5% phenylephrine drops in the superior fornix. Ptosis measurements are repeated after 10 minutes. Patients in whom the ptotic lid elevates due to stimulation of Muller's muscle are ideal candidates for posterior approach ptosis correction (conjunctival – mullerectomy surgery). 11. Tests to rule out myasthenia gravis: Fatigue test: The patient maintains fixation in upgaze for 30 seconds. In patients with myasthenia, the eyelid gradually drops down due to muscle fatigue.

agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
agent1_snippets.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by autoantibodies targeting AChRs or MuSK at the NMJ. Clinically, this condition presents as fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. The initial symptoms typically include extraocular muscle dysfunction, causing ptosis and diplopia. The condition may later affect the bulbar muscles, leading to dysphagia, and the limb muscles, causing generalized weakness. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: Electrophysiologic Tests: These are relevant in patients who are seronegative for antibody testing. Commonly employed tests for MG are the repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) test and single-fiber electromyography (SFEMG). Both the tests assess for conduction delays in the NMJ. Routine nerve conduction studies are usually performed to determine the functioning of the nerves and muscles before undertaking these tests.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2499
agent2_snippets.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” encompasses a diverse group of peripheral nerve and neuromuscular conditions. It is used to classify the nonspecific term “تكزز,” drug‑induced polyneuropathy (Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multifocal neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), multifocal neuropathy linked to collagen‑vascular disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), and myasthenic syndrome (وهن عضلي وبيل). By grouping these varied entities under “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians are reminded that many neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders fall outside the more common diagnostic categories, prompting comprehensive evaluation and tailored management for patients presenting with unexplained nerve or muscle dysfunction.

agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of nervous system is a diagnostic category that comprises a diverse group of neuropathic and inflammatory conditions. Within this category are the entities تكزز, اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد الكحولي (multifocal alcoholic neuropathy), اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة (severe neuropathy), اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين (chronic multifocal inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد (multifocal mononeuritis), الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflexive atrophic changes of the lower limb), and وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and peripheral weakness). Together, these conditions illustrate the breadth of disorders classified under other nervous system diseases, highlighting variations in etiology, severity, and anatomical distribution that are important for accurate diagnosis and targeted patient management.

Overall, the taxonomy connects genetic, metabolic, toxic, inflammatory, traumatic, and compressive insults to peripheral and cranial nerves, with clinical outcomes of pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
agent2_snippets.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) has a broad relationship with neurosurgery (جراحة الأعصاب), indicating that many surgical interventions target or involve this central organ system. It also serves as the anatomical site for procedures such as muscular neuro blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), highlighting its role in therapeutic techniques that modulate nerve‑muscle interactions. In addition, the nervous system is the anatomic site associated with other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, linking it to a spectrum of inherited and progressive disorders. Those hereditary and degenerative conditions make use of diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), suggesting that inflammatory processes of the brain and spinal axis are involved in their pathophysiology. Together, these connections underscore the central importance of the nervous system in surgical practice, procedural interventions, and the manifestation of certain hereditary and degenerative neurological diseases.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
agent2_snippets.content: Other disorders of the nervous system constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of specific neuropathic conditions. Within this category are “تكزز,” diabetic neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب السكري), idiopathic progressive neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب), multiple neuropathy associated with malignant disease (اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الخبيث), severe neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة), chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with root involvement (اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين), mononeuritis multiplex (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد), and lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي). By classifying these diverse entities under the umbrella of “Other disorders of nervous system,” clinicians can recognize that they share a common neuro‑anatomical focus while differing in etiology, severity, and clinical presentation, which aids in systematic evaluation, differential diagnosis, and tailored patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
agent2_snippets.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) is the anatomical site associated with various other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions. Interventions for these conditions may involve the procedure site of muscular neuro blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), indicating that targeted blockade techniques are relevant to nervous system pathology. The condition described as diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور) is linked to these hereditary and degenerative disorders, and valproate (Valproat) is specifically contraindicated in patients with this diffuse encephalitis. Recognizing the nervous system’s role as the primary anatomic site for hereditary and degenerative disorders, the application of muscular neuro blockade, and the contraindication of valproate in diffuse encephalitis guides clinical decision‑making and optimizes patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
agent2_snippets.content: The category “Other disorders of nervous system” serves as an umbrella classification that includes a diverse set of neurological conditions. It is used to classify “تكزز,” a term listed under this group, as well as “Polyneuropathie ten gevolge van geneesmiddelengebruik” (polyneuropathy resulting from drug use). Additional conditions classified within this category are “اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب” (progressive idiopathic neuropathy), “اعتلال الأعصاب بالغ الخطورة” (severe neuropathy), “اعتلال العصب والجذور المتعدد الالتهابي المزمن مزيل للميالين” (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), “الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي” (lower‑limb reflex sympathetic atrophy), “داء شاركو‑ ماري‑ توث” (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and “وهن عضلي وبيل” (muscular dystrophy/atrophy). By grouping these varied neuropathic and neuromuscular disorders together, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing that they share a common placement within the broader spectrum of nervous system disorders, facilitating systematic diagnosis, research, and management strategies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2758
agent2_snippets.content: Baclofen, a GABA‑B receptor agonist commonly employed as a muscle relaxant, may be used in the treatment of multiple sclerosis. In patients with multiple sclerosis, baclofen can help alleviate spasticity and muscle stiffness, which are frequent neurological manifestations of the disease. By reducing excessive neuronal firing in the spinal cord, baclofen contributes to improved motor function and comfort, making it a valuable therapeutic option for managing the motor symptoms associated with multiple sclerosis.
